speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph Carver in London, England. What happened after you went to live with your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We're talking now when I lived with this family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got bronchitis in the first week I stayed with these people. And then I got pneumonia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So these people were really very concerned. They were very nice people, very concerned [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and very good-hearted. They called a top consultant, who was a Czech Jewish doctor married to a non-Christian woman, Czech woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had four sons who all followed him into the ghetto, although they were not obliged. But they came with him. His name was Dr. Sinek. He was a professor at Prague University before the war. So he came, and he diagnosed bronchitis, pneumonia. And he prescribed a kind of a cod liver oil in liquid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, it's very difficult to be obtained. You can only obtain it in one of the exclusive shops for the directors. That was a kind of Ali Baba shop where you could get everything that was unobtainable in the ghetto, on special coupons given by the Germans for the directors. This meant that they had extra rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if somebody was ill and needed medication, you could get this as well. So they obtained the kind of cod liver extracts that was given to me in drops about four times a day. Then I was given some tablets made by Bayer, the famous German chemical combine. Also was unobtainable, but they had it for the directors. I was given that. And slowly, slowly-- the woman, the mother of the director was a nice, elderly lady. And she made some special broth for me. She looked after me. And slowly, slowly, I came back to my health. So when I was all right, they took me down to the factory, and they taught me the trade-- the basics of mechanics, how to repair a machine, what to look for, and so on. And I went out with other mechanics. I was, more or less, the mechanics' mate. I, I, I was carrying the tool bag. And we went from one factory to the other. We were in various factories, repairing these sewing machines. And when we had to go on the other side of the ghetto, we had to be very careful because we were crossing the bridge. We had to see how many meters away the guard was, so that we were not getting a bullet in our neck or in our chest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to work out the mathematics in seeing the distance that kept us away from him, and if we were on the other side, if he were looking up, if he was shooting at us, and how we should go. Should we go erect? Or should we go bowed down so that he wouldn't see the upper half of us until we came on the other side? So it was, more or less, a hide-and-seek game, a cat-and-mouse game. You had to evade all these border guards. And this went on for a little while.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I think I mentioned before that people were dying like flies in the ghetto. They had typhoid. They had dysentery. My aunts died. My uncles died. Cousins died. Everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: People around us-- if they were living with their own kind-- they would wake up in the morning. They would say, oh, we have been robbed. I haven't got my bread. Somebody ate my bread. Somebody ate my sugar. I haven't got this. I'm going to die now. Now, who can be the culprit? It's either the father or the mother or the son-- the son eating the father's ration or the father eating the son's ration or the daughter's ration. All this happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes people, when the father was dying or the mother was dying, they would take away their clothing and put their clothing on them. And they would try to get hold of their rations until it was reported. Because when the death was reported, automatically, that card ceased to function. But when it was found out that somebody used a ration card, and these people were caught, they went to prison. And although they were given a sentence by the Jewish judges, the sentence never materialized, because every time they needed people for deportation. Like the Germans said, they need 10,000 people. The ghetto, when it was locked up on the 1st of May, 1940, it had 320,000 Jews. Pre-war Jews were about 350,000, a third of the population. Lodz had about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 700-- 720-- or-- thousand population. Ethnic Germans were 150,000, Jews, 350,000. That makes it 500. The rest were all Poles. Now, these were the statistic, officially. They could be out for over a few thousand. It could be under or over. Nobody knows, because they didn't have a proper system to count in those days. And that was, I think, the 1936 census. So we go by those figures. And people were deported, like say, the Jewish underworld, which were obstructing the running of the ghetto. They were told by the Germans-- the Germans came in with fixed bayonets, and they took the whole lot out because they were the trouble makers. Then other trouble makers took over. They had strikes in the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the chairman went down and he said, what are you doing? You're gambling with your own lives. The Germans will come in. They will shoot us. They will kill us. Go back to work. Everything will be normal. We have to do this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he started appealing. And he said, look, I must have 10,000 children. Give me your children. At least, you see, if you're young, you can have more children, but I must have these children. At least, you see, we will play around. We will see. He tried to minimize everything and to keep the ghetto alive, to play around with the Germans. If they wanted 20,000, he was giving them 10,000. If they wanted 10,000, he says, I've only got 5,000. And so it went on and on. So in 1944, deportation took place en masse. Factory after factory was closed. People had to report. It came to my, to my factory as well. And the people I stayed with-- he was the director. He said to me, look, I have to go, and you have to go. You go now, maybe you will make a place for me, wherever you're going. Because nobody knew that we were going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. All the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think everyone was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought we were going to a labor camp. We were being deported to go to a labor camp inside Germany. That's what we thought we, we were going to. We had no idea about concentration camps, although we heard of it before the war. But we didn't imagine we would go to a concentration camp. We were working in factories, making ammunition, making uniforms for the German, making shoes. I was making store shoes. I was making leather shoes-- these high boots, which they were using on the Russian front and headgear and other stuff. So it never entered our minds that we were going to a concentration camp. And we had no knowledge about these camps or the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to register. And the day when I went, they gave me dollars. They gave me some of their own jewelry. They said, keep this in there, in case you need it to trade it in for something to eat, which was very, very [? munificent ?] from these people to give away from their own resources. This is the family you were staying with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family I was staying. So when I went to the gathering station where we all waited for transportation, and-- I was trying to talk to some other German-speaking people who were in charge of running these escapades. And the other people heard me talking to them. They said to me, please, take this for me. Maybe you can help me there. Take it. Take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I found I had lots of things in my pockets. Now when we went on these wagons, men and women-- What sort of wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: For transportation. Cattle wagons. And they put a bucket, two buckets there. There were women and children and young girls and old girls and grandmothers. As many people they could get in, like sardines, they went inside, in this wagon. And then the Germans sealed it. You couldn't look out. You couldn't see anything. And we were travelling the whole night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any idea how many people were in that wagon with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that when we were travelling in open wagons from Buchenwald to Czechoslovakia to Theresienstadt on our march, there were 80. So I assume it must have been about 80 as well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very, very cramped. And they had these two buckets where people relieved in them. First of all, it was stinking. The whole place. And what a sweat from the people. And some people had no showers, no bath, no facilities. It was absolutely suffocating, the whole place. And people cried, and people moaned, and people got wet because it was dark. There was no light. It was terrible. It was sheer bedlam. So when we eventually arrived in the morning when there was daylight-- we were travelling. We still didn't know where we were going. We, we could look out through the holes in the wagon. There were some holes. We could look through with our eyes and see that we were going through the countryside. Then, all of a sudden, we see a very big camp and people dressed up in pajamas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like striped, striped pajamas they were wearing. And barbed wire and people shouting. And the wagon train came to a halt. And the Germans with the dogs and the people who are working there, the prisoners, said, everybody out. Everybody out. And people went out. And whatever they had left-- some people took their suitcases. They were kicked and whipped. And on the way, they left it behind. So we came, and they sectioned us off. Men separately, children separately, and women separately. And Dr. Mengele, he was there selecting the people. I didn't know his name was Mengele. He was just a doctor. He had a whip in his hand. And this meant to the left and this meant to the right. I was dressed up in a boiler suit, a navy blue boiler suit. I had a screwdriver and a pair of pliers here in my front pocket. And in this pocket, I had a hammer and also screwdrivers and a pair of pliers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was the same height as when I was 10 years old, as when I left Germany. I never grew. I was the same height. So he said to me, and what are you doing? I said, I am ammunitions mechanic. How old are you? I said, 19. That way. So I went that way. What made you think to say you were that much older?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had anyone told you to say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It just came out. How, what, when-- I don't know. It saved my life. I said 19.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to march. And everything was in that camp. So we seen a sign and it said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, baths. We had to go there for a bath. So we went inside. We were told to undress and put our clothing in a bundle in front of us. So everybody was naked with a bundle. Then the prisoners-- because the Germans didn't come in. It were the prisoners, the Auschwitz prisoners who came in. They said, any jewelry, any money, any gold, you've got to put in the bucket. They brought buckets in lanes. Like, they had sections. I can't remember how many sections we were because-- oh, it must have been about 10 or 12 sections we were, this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whatever I had on me, I certainly didn't want to give it to the Germans or put it in the bucket for them. So with my hands, I made a hole in the sand. Very, very deep. And the-- I still had my tools. So I dug a big hole and put everything in it-- the money, the, the jewelry, everything in it. And I put the sand back over it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now they were calling out people to follow, one after the other. They said to go for a bath. There were so many people that it was suffocating, because when people are naked, one on top of the other, is when you watch animals running to get water, and there is a stampede. And people get killed in a stampede. That's exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was afraid in case these people who were surrounding me would tell the Germans that this fellow dug a grave and put it. So I ran in the front. I wanted to be in the front. I didn't want it to be behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran in the front, but I couldn't because, you see, you had to squeeze yourself through to get-- it's like going in zigzags through naked bodies to be in front. And there, we came into a proper bath house where they had showers, also supervised by prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality were these prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: The prisoners, they were Poles and Germans and Czechs. Also some were Jews. Some were non-Jews. All nationalities. First, we were washed clean. Then we were taken into another room to be shaved. They shaved all our hairs off. And we had already had our bath. Then we had to go through a disinfectant, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a disinfecting block, where they put a painting brush in the bucket of disinfectant, and the whole body was painted with this disinfecting liquid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And still marching around naked into a block where you were giving clothing, the striped clothing of the prisoners. They just looked at you. They didn't say, what size are you, anything like this. They just looked at you, gave you a bundle, which meant a shirt, a pair of trousers, a jacket, a coat, and a pair of shoes. No socks. No underwear, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when it was ill-fitting from the beginning, it would always be ill fitting, till the day they died. So they always said, it's far better to get something bigger because if you find something on the way, you can always stuff it in your trousers or you can wrap it around yourself. So always take something bigger. So we got something big. Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That? Some of the prisoners before they were dishing it out. So we were already dressed, and we came out. And we were told to stay in a different block. They marched us out, and we went in the block. So after two days, they came and took particulars-- our names, where we come from, our family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And because these people who were doing the writing were put in charge by the Germans, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they didn't understand the German which we were speaking, because they came from a different region. So mistakes were made in the spelling of the name, in the birth. Like say, for instance, if you say you were born on the 16th, they understood it was the 18th. Or the 18th, they understood it was the 28th or something like this. But it didn't really make any difference what they, what they wrote down. And then we were tattooed. When we had the tattoo, we were already considered cannon fodder. This means that we had to work for the Germans, for the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was tattooed here, on my left hand. It was B7674. But I took my tattoo out after the war when I was working for a very large English company and I had to travel in Europe. So I took this out, but I got the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] photograph of my tattoo, which was taken out in the Middlesex Hospital on Mortimer Street, London. But the mark is here. Shall I open it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you undo the button for me, please? You see, they didn't do a good job. It was here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] B7674. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, this was done under the National Health, so you can see, still, the marks here. It wasn't done properly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, while I was already tattooed and working outside the ghetto territory, picking up suitcases-- and the suitcases came from Hungarian Jews and Czechoslovakian Jews. They had their name on the suitcases from where they came from with lots of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we found food, like white bread and other preserved goods, which we ate because we were really starving hungry. And we had to look out that nobody was seeing it when we were taking this food. And we had to make, also, bundles-- bundles of shoes and the glasses and false teeth, artificial limbs and various clothing, handbags. All this, we had to sort out. And while I was marching, somebody called me. And I looked around. It was one of my uncle. He said, help me. He says, see what you can do for me. And this was the uncle who had the grocery shop. And I said to him, don't worry. Everything will be all right. You will be all right. Yes, he said. They took away my two boys. And he looked up to heaven. He knew that they were being cremated in the crematorium. They were burned there. He knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the crematorium at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We saw the crematorium, but we didn't go near it, because we were working for the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew, yes. We knew. We were acquainted with it, straight away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first job I had was in the chalk pits. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to go down into the chalk pits. One would, one would hold a sack. The other one would shovel it. And make-- at the end of sack, they had, like, a loop. You closed the loop so that the sack was full and was sealed. And the next in line had to take the 100 weight and carry it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as we had to change and rotate, it was my turn. They put this 100 weight on me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought I was going to drop dead straight away with the weight of it. I just couldn't. It, it was far too heavy. Not good enough that the weight is on me and I'm standing, but to walk-- we had to walk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] straight and then up, like, a little mountain. An impossible task, but it had to be made possible. And some people in front of me fell down. They were shot. Some other people had to come and scoop it-- not with the shovel, but with their hands. And the first one had to carry it. So I was afraid that this was going to happen to me because the guards were on the top. They had the machine guns, and they were looking down and calling us-- quick, quick, quick, you. And they used to have different names. Like either somebody had a long nose or somebody had small eyes. From so far away, they would see that people done the job. So when I came up and I let this 100 weight down properly, you see, so that it wouldn't split, I had to go down again. And luckily, my turn was to shovel, not to carry this because I already had done my job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the following day when they were calling out kommando section, I didn't go. They said, kommando section. So I saw different people. I said, oh, these are different people. They're not the same people that I was going. So I said to one, where are you going? Oh, they said, we are going on construction. We're doing construction work. I said, what is this? Oh, they said, we're constructing huts. It's more like joinery. So I went with these people. And we were constructing barracks for the SS. And all these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] various tools and supplies, they had written on it, Waffen-SS KZ Auschwitz and the number. So this was really the property of the SS. And we were working, constructing these barracks. So this went on for a few days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were beaten to death with the guns. Some were bayoneted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if anything was found, any food, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were taken out. Some were shot. Some were hanged. That was in the beginning because they said that we were stealing German property. We were not supposed to have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So just imagine, you coming back into the camp from work. You get your ration, which is a piece of bread. You get a little bit of soup. And I think they were giving us a little blob of margarine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is supposed to be food for 24 hours. Now, when you go, the following day, to work, you've got to have something to eat. Now, I knew that if I won't eat this, if I have it the next day, they either will beat me to death, shoot me, or kill me because they will say that I stole it. So I didn't want this to happen to me, so I always ate it. As I-- as I received it, I was eating it.

speaker: interviewee
text: People said, oh, you've got to keep something for tomorrow. What will you do? You're eating all this. You can die. You can die there. I said, well, you die, you die. I said, you can't help that. So we were given this ration, and we were eating this. Some ate it. Some didn't. And after a few weeks, they changed the kommandos. We had to go and work in different sections, different places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one high-ranking SS officer came, and he called out, who wants to volunteer to work in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] animal clinic? Now, he spoke German. A lot of them didn't understand, but when he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [GERMAN], which, in German, means animal clinic, I volunteered. And I went to work there.
